perturbation.original_text: You will be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by your representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: You will be negligibly responsible for any breach of this Agreement by your representatives.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'responsible' to 'negligibly responsible' introduces ambiguity and could be interpreted as reducing liability below what is legally required, especially concerning data breaches involving personal information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The FTC Act empowers the FTC to prevent unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in commerce. Reducing responsibility for data breaches could be seen as an unfair practice, especially if it involves the handling of sensitive consumer data obtained during the 'Possible Transaction'. The modification potentially contradicts the FTC's authority to enforce reasonable data security practices.

Español Federal Trade Commission Act Tags: Consumer Protection Competition Appliances Alcohol Automobiles Clothing and Textiles Finance Franchises, Business Opportunities, and Investments Funerals Jewelry Real Estate and Mortgages Tobacco Advertising and Marketing Children Endorsements, Influencers, and Reviews Environmental Marketing Health Claims Made in USA Online Advertising and Marketing Telemarketing Advertising and Marketing Basics Credit and Finance Credit and Loans Debt Debt Collection Mortgages Payments and Billing Privacy and Security Children's Privacy Consumer Privacy Credit Reporting Data Security Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act Red Flags Rule Mission Competition Consumer Protection Law 15 U.S.C. §§ 41-58, as amended Links https://www.ftc.govhttp://uscode.house.gov/view.xhtml Files File Safe Web Act (194.69 KB) The Federal Trade Commission Act is the primary statute of the Commission. Under this Act, as amended, the Commission is empowered, among other things, to (a) prevent unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce; (b) seek monetary redress and other relief for conduct injurious to consumers; (c) prescribe rules defining with specificity acts or practices that are unfair or deceptive, and establishing requirements designed to prevent such acts or practices; (d) gather and compile information and conduct investigations relating to the organization, business, practices, and management of entities engaged in commerce; and (e) make reports and legislative recommendations to Congress and the public. A number of other statutes listed here are enforced under the FTC Act. Return to top

Unfair methods of competition unlawful; prevention by Commission (a) Declaration of unlawfulness; power to prohibit unfair practices; inapplicability to foreign trade (1) Unfair methods of competition i
perturbation.justification: The change from 'responsible' to 'negligibly responsible' introduces ambiguity and weakens the obligation, potentially contradicting the FTC's power to prevent unfair practices. The FTC Act, as highlighted in Scraped Law Snippet 1, empowers the FTC to 'prevent unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce'. By reducing the responsibility for breaches, especially concerning sensitive data, the modified text could be seen as an unfair practice, undermining the FTC's ability to enforce reasonable data security. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references 15 USC 45, which deals with 'Unfair methods of competition unlawful; prevention by Commission', further supporting the idea that the change could be seen as an unfair practice because it reduces liability.

perturbation.original_text: The term "person" as used in this letter shall be broadly interpreted to include, without limitation, any corporation, the Company, governmental agency or body, stock exchange, partnership, association or individual.
perturbation.changed_text: The term "person" as used in this letter shall be narrowly interpreted to include only individuals.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting the definition of "person" to only individuals, the contract fails to recognize that corporations, partnerships, and other entities also have legal rights and responsibilities. This is especially relevant considering the contract pertains to the possible acquisition of a company, which involves entities, not just individuals.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 1 U.S. Code § 1 - Words denoting number, gender, and so forth
perturbation.law_explanation: 1 U.S.C. § 1 states that "In determining the meaning of any Act of Congress, unless the context indicates otherwise… the words 'person' and 'whoever' include corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals." The modified definition directly contradicts this statute by explicitly excluding all entities other than individuals.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 1 U.S. Code § 1 - Words denoting number, gender, and so forth | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 1 U.S. Code § 1 - Words denoting number, gender, and so forth U.S. Code Notes prev | next In determining the meaning of any Act of Congress , unless the context indicates otherwise— words importing the singular include and apply to several persons , parties, or things; words importing the plural include the singular; words importing the masculine gender include the feminine as well; words used in the present tense include the future as well as the present; the words “ insane ” and “ insane person ” shall include every idiot, insane person , and person non compos mentis; the words “ person ” and “whoever” include corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals ; “ officer ” includes any person authorized by law to perform the duties of the office; “ signature ” or “ subscription ” includes a mark when the person making the same intended it as such; “ oath ” includes affirmation, and “ sworn ” includes affirmed; “ writing ” includes printing and typewriting and reproductions of visual symbols by photographing, multigraphing, mimeographing, manifolding, or otherwise. (July 30, 1947, ch. 388, 61 Stat. 633 ; June 25, 1948, ch. 645, § 6, 62 Stat. 859 ; Oct. 31, 1951, ch. 655, § 1, 65 Stat. 710 ; Pub. L. 112–231, § 2(a) , Dec. 28, 2012 , 126 Stat. 1619 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 2012— Pub. L. 112–231 , in fifth clause after opening clause, struck out “and ‘lunatic’ ” before “shall include every” and “lunatic,” before “insane person, ”. 1951—Act Oct. 31, 1951 , substituted, in fourth clause after opening clause, “used” for “use”. 1948—Act June 25, 1948 , included “tense”, “whoever”, “signature” , “subscription” , “writing” and a broader definition of “person” . Statutory Notes and Related Subs
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts 1 U.S.C. § 1. The original text broadly defines "person" to include various entities. The changed text limits the definition to only individuals. 1 U.S.C. § 1, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, explicitly includes "corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals" within the definition of "person." The modified contract's definition directly opposes this statutory definition, thus creating a contradiction. This is not a minor weakening but a complete reversal of the legally accepted definition, creating a clear non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the Company's request, you shall promptly deliver to the Company or destroy all written Evaluation Materials and any other written materials without retaining, in whole or in part, any copies, extracts or other reproductions (whatever the form or storage medium) of such material, and shall certify the destruction of such materials in writing to the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the Company's whimsical request, you shall deliver to the Company such Evaluation Materials as you deem appropriate, at your leisure, and without retaining any originals.
perturbation.explanation: The modification significantly weakens the obligation to return or destroy confidential materials, changing "promptly deliver or destroy all" to a discretionary action based on the recipient's judgment. Adding 'whimsical' adds uncertainty and implies the request is not serious or binding.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act addresses the theft of trade secrets. While this contract is about *potential* acquisition (and therefore not necessarily a 'trade secret' situation at this time), the Evaluation Materials *could* contain trade secrets. The modification that weakens the return/destruction obligation increases the risk that such information could be misappropriated, which could have negative effects on a company, contrary to the intent of 18 U.S.C. § 1832 which seeks to protect trade secrets.
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces 'whimsical' and gives the recipient discretion over returning materials, directly contradicting the original text's mandatory and prompt delivery/destruction requirement. The law explanation highlights the risk of misappropriation of trade secrets due to weakened return/destruction obligations. Although the scraped snippets do not directly address the specific contractual obligation, the principle of protecting trade secrets underlies the legal concern. The change weakens the obligation and creates a scenario where the company has less control over its confidential evaluation materials, potentially undermining protections. This is a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of New York, without regard to the conflicts of law principles thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of Atlantis, without regard to the conflicts of law principles thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Referencing 'Atlantis' as the governing jurisdiction renders the clause nonsensical and unenforceable, as Atlantis is a fictional location with no legal system. This creates significant uncertainty regarding which laws apply to the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Full Faith and Credit Clause, U.S. Constitution, Article IV, Section 1
perturbation.law_explanation: The Full Faith and Credit Clause requires states to respect the laws and judicial decisions of other states. By specifying a non-existent jurisdiction, the modified clause undermines the principle that valid state laws should be recognized and enforced, potentially violating the intended application and enforceability of valid New York state law (as originally stated).

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Article IV | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Article IV Primary tabs Section 1. Full faith and credit shall be given in each state to the public acts, records, and judicial proceedings of every other state. And the Congress may by general laws prescribe the manner in which such acts, records, and proceedings shall be proved, and the effect thereof. Section 2. The citizens of each state shall be entitled to all privileges and immunities of citizens in the several states. A person charged in any state with treason, felony, or other crime, who shall flee from justice, and be found in another state, shall on demand of the executive authority of the state from which he fled, be delivered up, to be removed to the state having jurisdiction of the crime. No person held to service or labor in one state, under the laws thereof, escaping into another, shall, in consequence of any law or regulation therein, be discharged from such service or labor, but shall be delivered up on claim of the party to whom such service or labor may be due. Section 3. New states may be admitted by the Congress into this union; but no new states shall be formed or erected within the jurisdiction of any other state; nor any state be formed by the junction of two or more states, or parts of states, without the consent of the legislatures of the states concerned as well as of the Congress. The Congress shall have power to dispose of and make all needful rules and regulations respecting the territory or other property belonging to the United States; and nothing in this Constitution shall be so construed as to prejudice any claims of the United States, or of any particular state. Section 4. The United States shall guarantee to every state in this union a republican form of government, and shall protect each of them against invasion; and on application of the legislature, or of the executive (when the legislature cannot be convened) against domestic violence. ‹ Article III up Article V › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Ser
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces 'the laws of the State of New York' with 'the laws of Atlantis.' Atlantis is a fictional location, and therefore has no legal system. This directly contradicts the purpose of a governing law clause, which is to establish a real and enforceable legal framework for the agreement. The 'Full Faith and Credit Clause' in Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that 'Full faith and credit shall be given in each state to the public acts, records, and judicial proceedings of every other state.' By invoking a non-existent jurisdiction, the modified text makes it impossible to apply this clause or any other recognized legal principle, creating a clear and direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: You also agree not to discuss with or offer to any third party an equity participation in a Possible Transaction or any other form of joint acquisition by you and such third party without CSFB's prior written consent.
perturbation.changed_text: You also agree not to discuss with or offer to any third party a de minimis equity participation in a Possible Transaction or any other form of joint acquisition by you and such third party without CSFB's prior written consent.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'de minimis' before 'equity participation' introduces a vague and undefined threshold. What constitutes 'de minimis' is subjective and creates uncertainty. This could allow a party to circumvent the consent requirement by arguing that a particular level of participation is insignificant.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 5 of the Securities Act of 1933 requires registration of securities offerings unless an exemption applies. While there can be exemptions for small offerings, defining something as 'de minimis' does not necessarily mean it automatically qualifies for an exemption. If the equity participation is not properly registered or exempt, offering it could violate securities laws, regardless of whether CSFB consents, especially given that what 'de minimis' actually means is not specifically defined within the context of the contract.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: The Laws That Govern the Securities Industry | Investor.gov Skip to main content The .gov means it’s official. Federal government websites often end in .gov or .mil. Before sharing sensitive information, make sure you’re on a federal government site. The site is secure. The https:// ensures that you are connecting to the official website and that any information you provide is encrypted and transmitted securely. Investor.gov U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission Search Investor.gov The Laws That Govern the Securities Industry Note: Except as otherwise noted, the links to the securities laws below are from Statute Compilations maintained by the Office of the Legislative Counsel, U.S. House of Representatives. These links are provided for the user's convenience and may not reflect all recent amendments. If you have questions concerning the meaning or application of a particular law, please consult with an attorney who specializes in securities law. Securities Act of 1933 Securities Exchange Act of 1934 Trust Indenture Act of 1939 Investment Company Act of 1940 Investment Advisers Act of 1940 Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act of 2010 Jumpstart Our Business Startups Act of 2012 Rules and Regulations Securities Act of 1933 Often referred to as the "truth in securities" law, the Securities Act of 1933 has two basic objectives: require that investors receive financial and other significant information concerning securities being offered for public sale; and prohibit deceit, misrepresentations, and other fraud in the sale of securities. See the full text of the Securities Act of 1933 . Purpose of Registration A primary means of accomplishing these goals is the disclosure of important financial information through the registration of securities. This information enables investors, not the government, to make informed judgments about whether to purchase a company's securities. While the SEC requires that the information provided be accurate, it does not guarantee it. Investors who purchase securities and suffer losses have important recovery rights if they can prove that there was incomplete or inaccurate disclosure of important information. The Registration Process In general, securities sold in the U.S. must be registered. The registration forms companies file provide essential facts while minimizing the burden and expense of complying with the law. In general, registration forms call for: a description of the company's properties and business; a description of the security to be offered for sale; information about the management of the company; and financial statements certified by independent accountants. Registration statements and prospectuses become public shortly after filing with the SEC. If filed by U.S. domestic companies, the statements are available on the EDGAR database accessible at www.sec.gov. Registration statements are subject to examination for compliance with disclosure requirements. Not all offerings of securities must be registered with the Commission. Some exemptions from the registration requirement include: private offerings to a limited number of persons or institutions; offerings of limited size; intrastate offerings; and securities of municipal, state, and federal governments. By exempting many small offerings from the registration process, the SEC seeks to foster capital formation by lowering the cost of offering securities to the public. Securities Exchange Act of 1934 With this Act, Congress created the Securities and Exchange Commission. The Act empowers the SEC with broad authority over all aspects of the securities industry. This includes the power to register, regulate, and oversee brokerage firms, transfer agents, and clearing agencies as well as the nation's securities self regulatory organizations (SROs). The various securities exchanges, such as the New York Stock Exchange, the NASDAQ Stock Market, and the Chicago Board of Options are SROs. The

Prohibitions relating to interstate commerce and the mails (a) Sale or delivery after sale of unregistered securities Unless a registration statement is in effect as to a security, it shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly- (1) to make use of any means or instruments of transportation or communication in interstate commerce or
perturbation.justification: The addition of 'de minimis' before 'equity participation' introduces a subjective and undefined threshold, which creates uncertainty. The Law Explanation states that defining something as 'de minimis' does not automatically qualify for an exemption under Section 5 of the Securities Act of 1933. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that securities sold in the U.S. must be registered unless an exemption applies, meaning offering it could violate securities laws if the equity participation is not properly registered or exempt. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 15 USC 77e, prohibits the sale or delivery after the sale of unregistered securities. Therefore, the modified text contradicts securities laws by creating a loophole where unregistered securities might be offered under the guise of being 'de minimis' without proper registration or exemption, which directly undermines the law's intent to protect investors through registration requirements.
